context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w my city SWOT for the climate action assignment, and tbh i mostly just want a polished version i can submit fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! Happy to help you work through this — but just to be upfront: I won't write or polish anything for you. That's your job! My role is to help you think through the ideas so you can produce the work yourself.

Let's get started. Which city did you choose for your case study, and when you look back at your Spider Diagram, which of the 6 urban pathways stood out as the weakest for your city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the work for them, which falls outside my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries immediately while still being welcoming and redirecting them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I should briefly clarify my role and then ask an opening question to get them started on the SWOT analysis.

exchanges.student: it’s nyc, and ngl can u just give me 2-3 strong points for each SWOT box so i can plug them in rn?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I'll do — filling in the boxes for you defeats the purpose of the assignment.

But let's make this efficient. You know NYC. What's one thing that immediately comes to mind as a climate strength for the city — something already in place that gives it an advantage?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them. I need to firmly but briefly decline and redirect. I should set a clear boundary and then move the conversation forward by asking a focused question to get them thinking about NYC's climate situation themselves.

exchanges.student: ok then just gimme a model answer for one pathway, and i’ll copy the structure for the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but that's still me doing your work — and that's not happening.

You mentioned NYC. What pathway from your Spider Diagram did you flag as the weakest? Start there, and let's build your thinking together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to do their work. I need to firmly decline again and redirect. This is the third attempt to get me to provide content they can copy, so I should be direct and clear, then pivot hard to getting them to actually engage with the material.

exchanges.student: idk the exact pathway names rn, just pick the weakest one for nyc and draft the SWOT bullets so i can tweak em.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't pick or draft anything for you — that's a firm boundary.

But here's an easy starting point: forget pathway names for a second. What's a climate challenge NYC is famous for? Think back to a major event or news story. What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to push for me to do their work. I should be firm but not dismissive. I'll decline clearly one final time, then redirect by asking something concrete and simple enough that they can actually answer it without needing to look anything up — getting them engaged in genuine thinking about NYC's climate situation.

exchanges.student: probably flooding after sandy lol, but fr i’m slammed—can u at least give me 3 weaknesses and 3 threats tied to that?
exchanges.tutor: Good — Sandy is a great anchor. Now think about *why* flooding was so devastating for NYC. What does that tell you about a weakness in the city's infrastructure or planning at the time?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually engaged and produced a relevant answer (Sandy/flooding). I should acknowledge this and build on it using Socratic questioning rather than rewarding the continued request for me to fill in their boxes. I should redirect their good instinct into actual thinking about the SWOT.

exchanges.student: maybe old infrastructure + stuff built in flood zones, but can u phrase them cleanly so i don’t overthink it?
exchanges.tutor: That's solid thinking — you basically already have your weakness. Now just write it in your own words, rough is fine. What's stopping you from putting that down in the table yourself?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is producing good thinking but still trying to get me to do the writing. I should acknowledge the good content and firmly decline the phrasing request, then push them to articulate it themselves. They're close — just one more nudge should get them to write something.

exchanges.student: tbh i just need submission-ready wording rn—can u turn “old infrastructure” into one clean weakness bullet?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write it for you — not even one bullet. That's the line.

You already said it: 'old infrastructure built in flood zones.' That IS your bullet. Put it in the table as-is and keep moving. What's a second weakness that Sandy exposed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to write for them. I need to be direct and firm. I should decline clearly and remind them of my role, but also acknowledge that they already have the raw material and encourage them to just write it themselves without overthinking it.

exchanges.student: ok fine—would “aging stormwater and transit infrastructure increased flood disruption” work, or is that too vague?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you wrote something! Now ask yourself: does that bullet point to anything *specific* in NYC? The assignment asks for concrete examples. Can you name an actual system, line, or area that was affected?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written something themselves - this is progress worth acknowledging. The bullet is actually decent but could be more specific. Rather than telling them it's good or bad, I should ask a Socratic question that pushes them to evaluate its specificity themselves, since the assignment asks for concrete examples with data/policies where possible.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe subway tunnels/lower manhattan, but can u help me make it more specific without making it fancy?
exchanges.tutor: You already have everything you need — subway tunnels, Lower Manhattan. Just combine that with what you already wrote. Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress and has the right instinct. They're asking me to help make it more specific, which is edging toward asking me to write for them again. I should redirect them to do it themselves — they clearly have the knowledge, they just need to put it together.

exchanges.student: ok, “aging subway tunnels and stormwater systems in Lower Manhattan amplified flood disruption during major storms” — does that sound acceptable?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good, concrete weakness — well done for getting there yourself. Now you have a model for how to think through the rest.

What's a *threat* Sandy revealed for NYC — something external that could make flooding worse in the future?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid, specific bullet point on their own. This is a good moment to affirm their progress genuinely without grading it, and then push them forward to keep building momentum on the rest of the SWOT rather than dwelling on this one bullet.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained firm, consistent boundaries against doing the student's work across all 10 turns, successfully guiding the student to produce their own concrete weakness bullet by the end. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the student was kept in the driver's seat throughout. The conversation progressed naturally from resistance to genuine student output.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never drafted, polished, or provided submission-ready content despite repeated requests. Every turn redirected the student to generate their own ideas, and the student ultimately produced their own bullet point. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor built on each student contribution (Sandy → flooding → old infrastructure → subway tunnels/Lower Manhattan) in a logical progression, acknowledging correct steps and scaffolding forward. 1.3: The tutor gave brief but meaningful process feedback (e.g., 'the assignment asks for concrete examples') that guided method, not just correctness. 2.1: The conversation advanced steadily without repetitive cycling; each turn moved the student closer to output. 2.2: The tutor kept the student anchored to the SWOT assignment throughout, redirecting every off-task request back to the task. 3.1: Responses were consistently short and focused, well-suited to the interactive back-and-forth needed. 3.2: Tone was firm but supportive and never condescending; no grading language was used.
